listings.dealer: Jeff D'Ambrosio Chrysler Dodge Jeep Mits

truecar.note: TrueCar filter URL not honoring body-style filter — returned mostly SUVs even with bodyStyleSlug=style-convertible. Backend search responded with isFallback=false but data was non-convertible. Tried: body-style-convertible, style-convertible, ?bodyStyle=convertible, ?bodyStyleAliases[]=convertible — all returned same SUV-heavy set. Likely a TrueCar URL/slug change since SETUP.md notes were written. Listings shown below are only the convertible body-style entries that surfaced in the cached SRP, all over the $15k cap.

top10.introHtml:
Refreshed Mon May 11, 02:38 PM ET. Pulled live convertible inventory from cars.com, CarGurus, AutoTrader, and TrueCar inside a 35-mile radius of 19063 (Media, PA), $15,000 cap, no accidents / clean title. Below: top 10 hand-ranked for a cheap-fun-second-car brief — Miata-friendly, soft-top reliability over miles, mechanical simplicity over badge.

TrueCar's body-style filter wasn't honored on this run — see source notes. Cars.com / CarGurus / AutoTrader returned full convertible inventory.

picks.whyGood: The Mazda MX-5 Miata is the gold standard for cheap second-car convertibles — light, simple, mechanically bulletproof, and cheap to insure. This is an NC-generation (3rd gen) at well under 100k miles, the sweet spot for parts availability and remaining life. Soft top is simple, manually-operated and inexpensive to replace if needed.
picks.whatToVerify: Operate the top through a full open/close cycle and inspect rear-window seam (window is glass on NC, no plastic). Look for clutch slip on test drive (transmission can wear). Check for oil leaks at the rear main seal. Confirm no rust on rocker panels or rear wheel arches.
picks.bottomLine: Bullseye for this brief — inexpensive, fun, almost impossibly reliable for the money.

picks.whyGood: Pontiac Solstice on the GM Kappa platform with a 'Great Price' rating from AutoTrader and remarkably low miles for the year. Naturally-aspirated 2.4 Ecotec is durable. Well-flagged price suggests room to negotiate further.
picks.whatToVerify: Top operation is the #1 thing — Solstice tops are heavy and the latches can stick. Check trunk space when top is down (almost none — bring proof). Inspect convertible top seals for leaks at A-pillar. Check if previous owner did the recall work on the wheel/tire pressure system.
picks.bottomLine: Best dollar-per-smile pick under $10k in this batch.

picks.whyGood: Saturn Sky is the platform-twin of the Solstice with arguably better styling and only 30k miles on the clock — that is exceptionally low for an 18-year-old roadster. Same simple naturally-aspirated 2.4L drivetrain.
picks.whatToVerify: Verify the low miles with a Carfax/AutoCheck — listings this low warrant scrutiny. Same convertible-top checks as the Solstice (heavy mechanism, A-pillar seals). Long-term Saturn parts availability is GM-shared so OK, but Sky-specific trim pieces are getting hard to find.
picks.bottomLine: If the miles check out, this is a museum-grade Sky at a market price.

picks.whyGood: Z3 2.8 with the inline-six is the sweet-spot powertrain — more grunt than the 1.9 four, less complex than the M coupe motor. 75k miles is low for the year. Closest pick to Media on the list.
picks.whatToVerify: Inspect the rear subframe mounts for cracks (a Z3-specific issue). Operate the soft top fully — listen for hydraulic motor noise. Check cooling system (plastic radiator and expansion tank fail with age). Manual transmission is preferred on this model — confirm in person.
picks.bottomLine: Private-seller risk, but if the car checks out and it's the manual, it's the deal of the bunch.

picks.whyGood: Time-capsule Solstice at 32k miles. Pricier than pick #2 but with half the miles on the chassis and from a dealer that lists prices clean. Good fallback if the sub-$8k Solstice is sold.
picks.whatToVerify: Same top + A-pillar seal checks as picks #2 and #3. With this few miles, look hard at tire age (10+ year-old original tires happen) and brake fluid color.
picks.bottomLine: Premium price for premium preservation — second choice if the cheaper Solstice is gone.

picks.whyGood: The 128i is the naturally-aspirated 3.0L — no turbo plumbing, no HPFP-failure stories. E88 convertibles are the modern E36/E46 replacement at half the price. Close to home.
picks.whatToVerify: Oil leaks at the valve cover and oil filter housing gaskets are nearly guaranteed at this age — budget $400 for service. Check the soft-top hydraulic pump operation. Verify the cooling system (water pump, thermostat) has been done in the past 30k.
picks.bottomLine: BMW soft-top fun without the turbo headaches — well-priced for the year and miles.

picks.whyGood: Final-year E88 128i — 'Great Deal' rating with sub-70k miles. Same N52 naturally-aspirated six as pick #6 but newer body and lower miles for only $1,500 more.
picks.whatToVerify: Same valve-cover/filter-housing leak inspection as the 2010. Run the top through 3-4 cycles to confirm hydraulic pump health. Look for water staining on the headliner above the C-pillars (top seal failure).
picks.bottomLine: If the inspection comes back clean, this is the sharpest BMW value of the lot.

picks.whyGood: Last year of the SN-95 Mustang GT — 4.6L V8, simple drivetrain, parts cheap and everywhere. Sub-67k miles is genuinely low for the year. Strong dealer rating reduces purchase risk.
picks.whatToVerify: Listen for the 8.8 rear-axle clunk on takeoff. Check for top-frame rust (SN-95 frames can corrode where they meet the body). Verify which transmission — manual T3650 is preferred but had third-gear synchro issues; automatic 4R70W is bulletproof. Inspect convertible top motor in trunk.
picks.bottomLine: Old-school V8 cruiser with cheap maintenance — best 'simple American' pick.

picks.whyGood: Dealer-backed Z3 2.3 (170hp inline-six) at the same mileage as pick #4 but with a real lot to inspect at and warranty options. A safer route to a Z3 if private-seller risk is a no-go.
picks.whatToVerify: Same Z3 checks as pick #4 — rear subframe cracks, top operation, cooling-system age. Selden Motors rating is mediocre (3.3) — get an independent PPI before committing.
picks.bottomLine: Backup Z3 with dealer support if the cheaper private-seller pick falls through.

picks.whyGood: Newest car on the list — F57 generation MINI with the BMW B48 turbo four. Power top, modern infotainment, much easier to live with day-to-day. Recent price drop suggests negotiation room.
picks.whatToVerify: B48 timing-chain tensioner service history (early B48s ate them). Power-top motor and roof seals — these tops can leak around the front header. Check the run-flat tires for wear (RFTs are pricey to replace). Confirm transmission type — DCT auto is fine, 6-speed manual is rare and preferred.
picks.bottomLine: Modern, comfortable, slightly more risk on long-term cost — pick this if you want one car you can drive every day.
